title: Macramé dog collar
subtitle: An adjustable knotted cord dog collar in 3 mm cotton or hemp cord, sized for small to medium dogs
excerpt: A macramé dog collar is a practical introduction to working in the round with macramé: the square knot pattern produces a flat, firm strap that adjusts through a simple sliding-knot closure. This collar is sized for a 30-45 cm neck circumference and uses soft 3 mm cotton or hemp cord.
sourceNotes: Macramé dog collar pattern synthesised from general macramé knotting practice. The square knot flat strap with sliding-knot closure is a standard approach for adjustable macramé accessories.

glossaryTerms.definition: The basic macramé knot used to form the collar strap. Two working cords cross over two filler cords in alternating directions, producing a flat, balanced unit.
A pair of square knots tied around both collar ends, allowing the loop to expand and contract. Sliding the two knot units apart widens the collar; sliding them together tightens it.
The mounting knot used to attach cords to the D-ring. A cord folded in half and larks-headed onto the ring produces two working ends.
A flat, D-shaped metal ring used to attach a lead to the collar. The macramé strap is built directly onto the D-ring as a starting point.

attrs.title: Safety note
attrs.body: This collar is for everyday wear only. Remove it during play or when the dog is unsupervised. Do not use macramé cord for a collar on a dog that pulls hard on the lead.

content.text: A macramé dog collar is built as a flat strap of

. The collar fits necks from 30-45 cm: the sliding clasp adjusts the loop to the correct circumference. Use 3 mm cotton or hemp cord: softer than nylon cord, it is comfortable against the dog's coat. All cords are attached to the D-ring using

items.name: 3 mm cotton or hemp macramé cord

content.text: Cut 4 lengths of cord: 2 lengths at 130 cm each (working cords) and 2 lengths at 90 cm each (filler cords). Fold all 4 lengths in half and attach to the straight bar of the D-ring using lark's head knots: working cords on the outside, filler cords in the centre. You now have 8 cord ends hanging below the ring: 4 working cord ends on the outside and 4 filler cord ends in the centre.

Work a series of square knots using the 2 outer working cords and 2 inner filler cords on each side (4 working, 4 filler total). For each square knot: take the left outer working cord over the 4 inner filler cords and behind the right working cord; bring the right working cord behind the fillers and up through the loop on the left. Repeat with the right cord going over first for the second half of the knot. Continue tying square knots until the strap measures 50 cm from the D-ring.

Bring the free end of the strap back alongside the D-ring end, forming a loop of approximately 40 cm circumference. Cut a separate 30 cm length of cord. Hold both strap ends parallel and use the separate cord to tie 4 square knots around both strap sections together, treating both straps as the filler cords. This forms the sliding clasp: the two knot units slide along the strap to tighten or loosen the collar loop. Trim the clasp cord ends to 1 cm and tuck them into the knot body.

Trim the strap tails at each end to 3-4 cm below the last square knot. Unravel the cut ends to form a short fringe. Apply a small amount of fabric glue or fray-check liquid to the very tip of each unravelled end if the cord is prone to unravelling during wear.

items.problem: The strap is twisting rather than lying flat
items.cause: The square knots were not worked consistently: one direction was favoured over the other
items.fix: A true square knot alternates direction: left-over first, then right-over-first. If both halves are done in the same direction, the strap will spiral. Count carefully: each complete square knot is two half-knots in opposite directions.

items.problem: The sliding clasp does not hold position and slips too easily
items.cause: The clasp knots are too loose or there are too few of them
items.fix: Add 2 more square knots to the clasp section (6 total) and pull each knot very firmly. The clasp should slide when deliberately adjusted but grip under normal tension.

items.problem: The collar is too short for the dog's neck
items.cause: The strap was knotted too tightly, taking up more cord length than expected
items.fix: Measure the dog's neck and add 10 cm before starting to knot. Knot a 5-knot test section and measure how much cord was consumed per knot, then calculate the total strap length needed.
